personSummary.value: Former weekly temple visitor who feels prepared for meals when his prayer beads are located, usually in his jacket or left pocket. Temple bell recordings, quiet counting with beads, and yes-or-no prompts support care. Dinner or medication can stall if trays arrive before the beads are found. Open questions are less reliable than simple yes-or-no choices.

value.description: Dinner setup before locating prayer beads recently led to pushing the tray away.
Prayer bead check and yes-or-no prompts supported evening medication acceptance.

value: Check that prayer beads are in his left pocket before dinner or medication.
Use yes-or-no prompts instead of open questions during morning or evening care.
Play a short temple bell recording or count quietly with the beads before meals.

citations.quote: prayer beads were in his left pocket

value: Dinner tray arriving before prayer beads are located.
Open-ended questions during care when he is already quiet or looking down.

citations.quote: Dinner tray arrived before prayer beads were located

value.approach: Locate the prayer beads first, place them in his left pocket or hand, then serve the tray.

citations.quote: Meal resumed after staff found the beads

value.approach: Use yes-or-no prompts and a quiet tone, with the beads available.

citations.quote: bell sound or quiet counting with the beads

handoffBrief.value: Hiroshi Kondo needs prayer beads located before dinner or medication, responds to temple bell or quiet counting cues, and answers yes-or-no prompts more reliably than open questions.

citations.quote: Ate better after staff found the beads

value.claim: Watch dinner refusal when the prayer bead routine is skipped.
